title: 30 days running a long-only mean reversion algo: 54 closes at 100% win rate, 36 positions still open. Why "win rate" is the worst way to judge this.
author: JrichCapital
selftext:
Posted something similar on another sub and got sharp feedback about why 100% win rate is misleading on a long-only algo. The feedback was right. Sharing the full picture here because this sub gets the nuance.



\## Today's snapshot

\- Trades closed: 54

\- Realized win rate: 100%

\- Realized PnL: +$316.44

\- Avg per trade: +$5.86

\- Open holdings: 36

\- Floating PnL: +$80.17

\- Committed capital: $1,097.15

\- Total equity: $2,251.19

(You can verify now on Live Stream)



\## Why the win rate is the wrong thing to celebrate

The system is hardcoded to never realize a loss. If price reverts, position closes green. If it does not, position stays open. So "100% win rate" really means "100% of trades that hit an exit, hit a green exit". The ones that did not revert are still sitting on the book.

Right now that book is +$80 floating. In a prolonged downtrend that floats negative and stays there.



\## The metrics I actually watch

Win rate is cosmetic. These matter more:

\*\*Time underwater per position\*\*: how many days does a position sit open before hitting an exit level? If the average is above 30 days, the system is hoarding dead capital, not cycling it.

\*\*Equity curve slope during drawdowns\*\*: specifically how fast the floating PnL line bleeds on bad days. Any long-only system with no short leg will draw down. The question is how fast and how deep.

\*\*Committed vs free capital ratio\*\*: right now at 48% deployed. In a bad week that goes to 90%+ and the system becomes fully occupied waiting for reversion instead of taking new entries.



\## How this differs from a standard DCA bot

Got this question on another sub and it is worth answering here too. A 3Commas style DCA bot executes a preset. You pick the pair, the deviation, the safety orders, the TP, the volume scale. The bot runs the config you gave it. You are the signal layer.

My system picks the pair. Every 15 seconds it scans 300+ pairs through a multi-condition exhaustion filter and fires entries only on pairs that pass. The bot is the signal layer. That is the actual difference, for better or worse.

Exit side is a 3-level grid with trailing on the last level, not a single TP. And the floor rule (no loss realization) is enforced at the engine, not a user toggle you can flip.



\## The honest structural weakness

If BTC/ETH and a basket of alts enter a synchronized multi-month secular downtrend, the system bleeds on open book until either reversion plays out (slow) or committed capital fully deploys and the engine idles waiting.

No hedge leg. No short side. Long only. That is the real risk, not the win rate.



\## Questions for this sub

\- Anyone running a similar architecture? How do you measure time underwater? Simple "days since entry" or something volatility adjusted?

\- If you solved the drawdown ceiling on a long-only, what did you add? Short leg, hedge via perps, or just deeper capital reserves?

\- Do you size entries by volatility regime or keep sizing flat?



Happy to go deeper on architecture at whatever level makes sense. Not here to shill. Just compare notes with people who actually build this stuff.

replies.author: JrichCapital
replies.body:
Fair question, worth unpacking because two different things are getting conflated.

First, what's being sold. I'm not selling the edge, I'm selling the infrastructure that runs it. Engine, scanner, dashboards, execution layer, 24/7 uptime. The signals and the logic stay with me and keep running on my own capital in parallel. Users get access to the platform, not a copy of the strategy in a PDF.

Think Bloomberg Terminal or TradingView. They don't sell you their trades, they sell you the rails to run yours. The fact that Bloomberg also operates trading desks doesn't cancel the terminal business. Same logic here.

Second, why run both. Two fundamentally different businesses stacked on the same stack:

1. Trading your own book. Compounds with capital, but hits walls fast. Market impact moves prices against you as orders scale. A system making 15-20% a year on $10k makes $1,500-2,000. On $10M it doesn't make $1.5M because your own orders are moving the pair.

2. SaaS on the infrastructure. Zero marginal cost per user. Revenue scales with seats, not capital. 500 users at $29/mo is $174k/year recurring, fully decoupled from what the market does. If BTC goes sideways for a year, the SaaS business still exists.

One funds the other and proves it works live. The stream shows real capital executing on the same platform anyone can buy, that's the product demo. Renaissance, Jane Street, Two Sigma all run proprietary books AND sell or license tech. Standard playbook.

And "indefinite amount of money" is not what I claimed. The OP has a section titled "honest structural weakness" explaining exactly where the drawdown risk lives. Might be worth reading before the next emoji wave.

replies.author: Redas17
replies.body:
1) You didn't really reply to my statement.
2) 100% winrate is unlimited money.
3) It's nothing to do with Bitcoin, because you can move your Algo into any other asset, there is nothing special about BTC, same CFD as any other.
4) Listen to yourself, you want to sell took that has 100% winrate for 29$ a month 🤣 What's the point if you really had working Algo?)))
5) You obviously know what you are doing (scamming), but your scam is so childish, why did you state 100% winrate? If you stated 51-52% that was already enough for unlimited money for 29$ a month 🤣🤣🤣

replies.author: JrichCapital
replies.body:
1. "100% winrate is unlimited money" is wrong on its face. Read the post. 100% closed trade win rate, 36 positions still open, +$80 floating. If even one of those open positions never reverts, the "unlimited money" math collapses. I literally wrote a whole section about this called "honest structural weakness". You skipped it.

2. "Move the algo to any asset" is also wrong. Mean reversion depends on the statistical properties of the underlying, volatility regime, liquidity, and tick size. An algo tuned for BTC/ETH majors on Binance Spot does not transplant to gold CFDs or forex without full re-parameterization and usually a different signal model. If you think all markets are the same, you have never run a live book.

3. "Why sell a 100% algo for $29" was answered in the thread by a 3Commas representative who explained it better than I could. I'm not selling the algo, I'm selling the infrastructure that runs it. The signal logic stays with me. Users get the execution layer, like Bloomberg Terminal or TradingView. Neither of those companies sells you their trades either.

4. "Why state 100% if 51% is enough" is the line that gives you away. A 51% win rate on unleveraged spot with no stop losses and a 3-level grid exit does NOT produce unlimited money. It produces a slow grinder that gets annihilated the first time a position sits underwater for 6 months. The whole architectural point of the no-loss floor is that win rate stops being meaningful. It forces you to measure drawdown and time underwater instead. Which is exactly what the post argues.

You are arguing against a version of the post you did not read. That's fine, it happens, but the emojis were doing more work than the text is.

There’s a reason for the 100% win rate - it’s because it holds positions open until they’re closed. He isn’t saying it’s a good thing. He’s saying it’s a negative. 

You need critical thinking skills!!??

replies.author: jabberw0ckee
replies.body:
I don’t get why people think hoarding an idea and not sharing it is a good thing and anyone who has a real edge should keep it a secret. 

Rubbish.

My algo has an 83% win rate and a Sharpe ratio of 6.547 and I made it available to everyone for free. Some people truly want to help others. It is not true that just because someone wins a trade someone else lost. The stock market doesn’t work that way. It’s a fallacy to think that way. 

Stockkit.ai

comments.author: Gyro_Wizard
comments.body: One of my accounts does something similar with a rules based bot. I have a scanner that I use to pick stocks once a week. I then allow calls and puts to be purchased for that underlying and try to close everything out by the end of day.

replies.author: JrichCapital
replies.body:
Solid setup. Intraday close on options is a cleaner risk profile than what we do on spot, since theta forces you to exit regardless of where price goes. The open-book drag we deal with on mean reversion doesn't exist if you're flat by end of day.

Curious about the scanner side though. Weekly stock picks with calls and puts both enabled suggests you're directionally neutral at pick time and let the week tell you which side to hunt. Is the scan filtering for IV rank, unusual options flow, earnings catalysts, or pure technical setups on the underlying? And are you sizing calls/puts equally or biasing based on underlying momentum?
